fact: In the Northern Hemisphere, daylight is growing by about 3 minutes every single day around this time, which means you get an extra 21 minutes of sunlight compared to a week ago!
Skunks are typically waking up from their winter naps right around now in northern climates and getting ready to find mates - which means more stinky surprises in backyards!
Tree sap starts flowing like crazy around February 28 in maple trees, which is why maple syrup season is in full swing in places like Canada and Vermont.
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around this date - it's like nature's compass is getting ready to reset for spring!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, February 28 marks the end of their hottest summer days, and autumn is about to begin.
Earthworms start wiggling closer to the surface around this time in northern climates because the ground is thawing, making robins super happy about all the squishy snacks!
February 28 falls exactly 31 days before the spring equinox, making it like a countdown timer to when day and night become perfectly equal.
Pussy willow buds get fuzzy and fat right around this date in temperate regions, looking like tiny gray kittens stuck on tree branches.
When the moon is full on February 28, it's called the 'Snow Moon' because it typically marks the last big snowfall time in many northern places.
Chipmunks and squirrels often start acting extra crazy around now because they're burning through their winter fat stores and getting desperately hungry.
In cold places, ice on ponds and lakes starts making weird creaking and groaning sounds around this time as it begins to weaken and shift.
